title: Nowruz Countdown
subtitle: Norouz • نوروز • Persian New Year
blurb: Nowruz, also spelled Norouz and written نوروز in Persian, marks the first day of spring and the beginning of the Persian New Year at the exact moment of the vernal equinox, with exact Tahvil time shown in local time, Tehran time, and UTC.
haftsin_sabzeh: Sprouted wheat, barley, or lentil greens, grown in the weeks before Norouz as a living symbol of spring. Sabzeh represents rebirth, renewal, and the reawakening of nature after winter. On Sizdah Bedar, the sabzeh is released into flowing water, carrying away the past year’s misfortunes.
haftsin_sib: A fresh red apple, one of the oldest cultivated fruits on the Iranian plateau. Sib symbolizes health and beauty, and its presence on the table is a wish for the well-being of family members in the coming year.
haftsin_sir: Whole garlic bulbs, long revered in Persian traditional medicine. Sir represents health, protection, and the warding off of illness and evil — rooted in ancient Zoroastrian beliefs about purity and spiritual safeguarding.
haftsin_senjed: The dried fruit of the oleaster tree, with a sweet, floury taste. Senjed symbolizes love and wisdom — its fragrant blossoms are said to stir the heart, while in traditional medicine it was prized for strengthening the mind.
haftsin_samanu: A rich, sweet pudding made from germinated wheat, prepared through hours of patient, communal cooking. Samanu symbolizes affluence, fertility, and the sweetness that comes from perseverance. Its laborious preparation embodies togetherness and shared blessing.
haftsin_somaq: Crushed sumac berries with a deep crimson color. Somaq evokes the color of sunrise and symbolizes patience and the triumph of light over darkness — a reminder to endure hardship with forbearance, trusting that brighter days lie ahead.
haftsin_serkeh: Aged vinegar, traditionally made from grapes or apples over many years. Serkeh symbolizes patience, age, and wisdom born from life experience — just as vinegar develops its character through a long, slow transformation.
haftsin_ayeneh: A mirror placed at the center of the Haft-Sin table, symbolizing self-reflection, purity, and the light of truth. At the moment of Tahvil, families look into the mirror — a tradition signifying the start of the new year with clarity and honest intentions.
haftsin_mahi: Live goldfish swimming in a bowl of water, representing life, vitality, and the flowing movement of time. Their vibrant red color symbolizes energy and joy, while their constant motion evokes the ever-turning cycle of the year.
haftsin_sham: Lit candles on the Norouz table represent light, enlightenment, and the warmth of happiness. Rooted in Zoroastrian reverence for fire, each flame symbolizes the light of life and hope. Traditionally, one candle is placed for each child in the family.
haftsin_tokhmmorgh: Painted and decorated eggs, often one per family member. They symbolize fertility, new life, and the creative power of nature. This ancient tradition celebrates the potential for new beginnings that reawakens every spring.
haftsin_sekkeh: Gold or silver coins placed on the table, symbolizing prosperity, wealth, and the hope for financial well-being. In many families, elders give coins as Eidi — new year’s gifts — to younger members, sharing the wish for abundance.
dormant_sizdah_bedar: We hope you enjoyed Sizdah Bedar — the thirteenth day of Norouz, when families picnic in nature and release their Sabzeh into running water, symbolizing the renewal of life.
dormant_next_countdown: The countdown to the next Norouz will begin soon.
